region_of_origin: China (Palace cuisine tradition); served in Singapore

ontology.foodon_gap_note: No specific FoodOn term exists for rose-flavoured Chinese flaky pastry. FOODON_03301710 (pastry food product) used as closest parent.
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 A039V (Pastries and cakes) is the closest parent category. No specific code exists for Chinese rose flaky pastry.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No suitable SNOMED CT concept exists for this specific Chinese pastry type.

serving.natural_language_portion: One standard serving is 6 pieces (approximately 120 g), as sold at See Ya Tonight restaurant in Singapore.

nutrient_source.title: Chinese Mooncake / Flaky Pastry with Sweet Filling — USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy composite; adapted for rose-paste filled Chinese shortcrust pastry
nutrient_source.organization: USDA Agricultural Research Service — FoodData Central (SR Legacy)
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: No exact entry for rose-flavoured Chinese flaky pastry exists in HPB Singapore Foodo, USDA FoodData Central, or FAO/INFOODS SEA databases. Values are estimated by compositing USDA SR Legacy data for: (1) Chinese-style flaky pastry shell (wheat flour, lard, sugar — USDA NDB 18033 / 18334 analogues) and (2) rose jam / rose paste filling (high-sugar fruit preserve analogue, USDA NDB 19297). Macro proportions estimated as approximately 55% pastry shell and 45% rose-paste filling by weight. All values are medium-confidence estimates. A direct laboratory analysis or HPB-registered entry would supersede these figures.

gi_source.title: Glycemic index of sweet pastry and biscuit products — analogous estimate based on shortcrust pastry and sweet jam-filled pastry entries
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for Chinese rose flaky pastry. GI estimated at 65 (medium) based on University of Sydney GI Database entries for shortcrust pastry (GI ~59–67) and sweet jam-filled pastry products (GI ~65–67). The high sugar content of the rose-paste filling supports a medium GI estimate. Glycemic load per serving calculated as: (GI × available carbohydrate per serving) / 100 = (65 × 70.8 g carbohydrate in 120 g serving) / 100 ≈ 46.0.

hypertension.key_factor: Moderate sodium content (~95 mg per 100 g)
hypertension.guidance: Rose Crisp contains approximately 114 mg sodium per 6-piece serving (120 g), which is modest; individuals with hypertension may consume it occasionally but should account for total daily sodium intake from other sources.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: High refined carbohydrate and sugar content with medium glycemic index (GI 65) and high glycemic load per serving (~46)
type2_diabetes.guidance: With approximately 70.8 g of carbohydrate and a glycemic load of ~46 per 6-piece serving, Rose Crisp poses a significant postprandial glucose challenge; individuals with Type 2 Diabetes should limit portion size to 1–2 pieces and pair with a protein or fibre source to blunt glycemic response.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Saturated fat from lard or shortening (~7.8 g per 100 g) and moderate trans fat (~0.2 g per 100 g)
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: The saturated fat content from traditional lard-based pastry shell and the presence of trace trans fats are relevant for cardiovascular risk; individuals with cardiovascular disease should limit consumption to occasional small portions and prefer versions made with unsaturated vegetable oils.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Rose Crisp (玫瑰酥, Méiguī Sū) is a traditional Chinese palace-style flaky pastry dessert characterised by its distinctive rose fragrance and a contrasting crispy outer shell with a soft, sweet rose-paste filling. The pastry is made using a layered Chinese shortcrust technique — wheat flour is combined with lard or vegetable shortening to create a laminated dough that bakes into delicate, flaky layers. The filling consists of rose jam or rose paste, typically made from preserved rose petals and sugar, imparting a floral aroma and a moderately sweet flavour. In Singapore, it is sold as a set of 6 pieces per portion and is associated with upscale Chinese restaurant dessert menus. Nutritionally, Rose Crisp is energy-dense at approximately 418 kcal per 100 g, with a significant carbohydrate load (59 g per 100 g, of which 28 g is sugar) and moderate fat content (18.5 g per 100 g, predominantly from the pastry shell). The glycemic index is estimated at 65 (medium), but the glycemic load per standard 6-piece serving is high (~46), making it a significant postprandial glucose challenge. It is not suitable for frequent consumption by individuals managing Type 2 Diabetes or cardiovascular disease. The dish contains gluten and is not suitable for those with wheat allergy or coeliac disease. It is generally considered vegetarian but may not be vegan or halal depending on whether lard is used in preparation.
common_user_queries: Is Rose Crisp suitable for diabetics?
How many calories in Rose Crisp?
What is the GI of Chinese rose pastry?
Chinese palace dessert with rose flavour

confidence_reason: No exact entry for rose-flavoured Chinese flaky pastry was found in HPB Singapore Foodo, USDA FoodData Central, or FAO/INFOODS SEA databases. Nutrient values are estimated by compositing USDA SR Legacy data for analogous Chinese-style flaky pastry shell and rose-paste filling components. GI is an analogous estimate from the University of Sydney GI Database based on comparable shortcrust and jam-filled pastry products. A direct laboratory analysis or an HPB-registered entry would upgrade confidence to high.
notes: 1. Nutrient values are medium-confidence estimates derived from USDA SR Legacy analogues for Chinese flaky pastry shell and rose-paste filling; no exact database entry exists for this specific product. 2. Vitamin A (vitamin_a_mcg_rae) is -1 as it is not reported in the analogous source entries used for estimation. 3. Vitamin B6 (vitamin_b6_mg) is -1 as it is not reported in the analogous source entries. 4. Vitamin B12 (vitamin_b12_mcg) is -1 as this is a plant-based pastry product and B12 is not expected to be present in meaningful quantities; not reported in source. 5. Folate (folate_mcg_dfe) is -1 as it is not reported in the analogous source entries. 6. Vitamin D (vitamin_d_mcg) is -1 as it is not reported in the analogous source entries. 7. Vitamin E (vitamin_e_mg) is -1 as it is not reported in the analogous source entries. 8. Selenium (selenium_mcg) is -1 as it is not reported in the analogous source entries. 9. GI of 65 is an analogous estimate from University of Sydney GI Database entries for shortcrust and jam-filled pastry; no direct measurement exists. 10. Halal status is uncertain as traditional preparation may use lard; restaurant-specific verification is required. 11. Serving size of 120 g (6 pieces) is based on the field survey listing description of '6 Pcs' and a reasonable per-piece weight estimate of approximately 20 g each for a Chinese flaky pastry of this type.
